attributes.stem:
A previously well 34-year-old woman attends her general practice with a one month history of recurrent episodes of right upper quadrant pain. The pain is triggered by fatty meals, last for 30 minutes and then resolves of its own accord. There is no history of urinary symptoms or vaginal bleeding. Her only medication is the oral contraceptive pill.

On examination, her temperature is 36.5°C, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, heart rate 60/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and respiratory rate 12/min. Her abdomen is not distended and is not tender to palpation. The general practitioner arranges an ultrasound and various laboratory investigations.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following laboratory investigations is the most relevant?
attributes.explanation:
Liver Function Tests (LFTs) are a group of markers including conjugated bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time and four liver enzymes, namely alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), gamma-glutamyl transaminase (GGT), and alkaline phosphate (ALP). Albumin and prothrombin time reflects the hepatic synthetic function. When interpreting LFTs it is useful to classify them into 2 patterns:

  • hepatocellular: predominant elevation of ALT and AST. AST/ALT ratio of ≥1 may indicate alcohol-induced hepatitis whereas AST/ALT<1 may indicate drug or viral causes. Markedly elevated transaminases can be seen with acute viral infections, autoimmune causes, drugs, and Wilson disease

  • cholestatic: predominant elevation of conjugated bilirubin, GGT, and ALP. Common causes are cholelithiasis and choledocholithiasis

The history of recurrent postprandial right upper quadrant pain is highly suggestive of biliary colic. Hence imaging the biliary system by ultrasound is the next step. In the presence of gallstones, any abnormalities of the liver function could reflect stones within the biliary tree. An ultrasound examination might pick up duct stones, but if there was any doubt, magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) will provide good definition of the ductal system.

Neutrophil count and c-reactive protein are commonly elevated in inflammation or infection. Cholecystitis, cholangitis, and hepatitis are important differentials to consider with right upper quadrant pain. However, these are less likely given that the patient is well without any fever, tachycardia, abdominal pain or tenderness.

Ectopic pregnancy should be considered in any women of reproductive age presenting with abdominal pain. Serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin is elevated in pregnancy. This diagnosis is less likely in this case due to the absence of vaginal bleeding and the use of oral contraceptive pill. The length of the history and the site of the pain makes a gynaecological cause for this patient's problem very unlikely.

Serum lipase is an enzyme produced by the pancreas. It is usually measured with another enzyme, amylase to detect pancreatitis which commonly presents with epigastric or left upper quadrant pain. Elevation of lipase or amylase to three times or greater than the upper limit of normal is characteristic of pancreatitis.
learningPoints: ALP, GGT, and conjugated bilirubin are more significantly elevated in cholestasis
ALT and AST are more significantly elevated in hepatocellular damage
Biliary colic is a constant dull discomfort located at the right upper quadrant and typically precipitated by a fatty meal
references: Kim WR. Clinical implications of short-term variability in liver function test results. Gastroenterology. 2008 Sep;135(3):1010–1. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18691585/
Grimpen F, Pavli P. Rational investigation of upper abdominal pain. Aust Fam Physician. 2008 Aug;37(8):602–7. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18704205/
Zantuck N, Wong M-L, Mackay S. Surgical causes of upper abdominal pain. Aust Fam Physician. 2008 Aug;37(8):614–8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18704208/
Crawford M. Biliary pain work-up and management in general practice. Australian Family Physician. 2013;42(7). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/july/biliary-pain
